title: 9 Hours vs. 527 Days: How Focused Sunlight Breaks Spaceflight's Oldest Tradeoff
hook: Over 1.2 million new satellites were filed for orbit in the last 18 months. Every one of them will need to move — dodge debris, change altitude, reach a servicing depot. For 50 years, engines forced a binary choice: move fast and die young, or move efficiently at a glacial pace. This explainer follows one job: pushing a 500 kg spacecraft from low Earth orbit to geostationary orbit, a 5,000 m/s velocity change we'll call the GEO run. One equation, \(v_e ∝ \sqrt{T/M}\), lets an engine heated by mirrors do it in 9 hours. The most efficient existing thruster needs 527 days.

parts.title: The trap: why moving in space is expensive
parts.lede: Before the new engine makes sense, you need the three rules every spacecraft obeys: thrust is thrown mass, maneuvers have fixed prices, and propellant costs grow exponentially. Together they create a 50-year-old dead end.

parts.title: The new engine: sunlight as the heat source
parts.lede: The trap has one exit: raise exhaust velocity without combustion. Here's the physics that permits it, and the hardware — mirrors, a heat exchanger, a nozzle — that builds it.

parts.title: Head to head on the GEO run
parts.lede: Now we race. Same 500 kg spacecraft, same 5,000 m/s job, three engines — compared on time, cost, and readiness, with the paper's actual numbers.

parts.title: Why 1980 failed and 2026 works
parts.lede: The physics was validated in 1979 and then shelved for four decades. Three technologies had to mature — and a million-satellite sky had to create the demand.

scenes.part: The trap: why moving in space is expensive
scenes.title: A rocket moves by throwing mass overboard, and the mass runs out
scenes.teaches: Thrust is momentum exchange with expelled propellant — \(F = \dot{m}·v_e\) — so a spacecraft's motion is bounded by the finite mass it carries to throw.
scenes.caption: There is nothing to push against in vacuum: the spacecraft accelerates only by expelling its own propellant, with force \(F = \dot{m}·v_e\). Watch the tank — when it empties, no amount of onboard power can change the trajectory.
scenes.visualMetaphor: Our 500 kg spacecraft (the vehicle for the whole explainer) floats on a 1D track. A slider sets exhaust velocity \(v_e\); a play button fires the engine, visibly ejecting a stream of propellant dots backward while the spacecraft drifts forward. Readouts: thrust \(F = \dot{m}·v_e\), tank level, spacecraft speed. Predict-before-reveal: 'The tank just hit zero and the batteries are full — press fire. What happens?' Answer: the dots stop, thrust drops to 0 N, velocity freezes.

animatedVariable.name: exhaust velocity \(v_e\)
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: Ejected dots leave faster, the thrust readout scales linearly (1 kg/s at 3,000 m/s → 3,000 N), and the spacecraft reaches a higher final speed from the same tank.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: Expelling 1 kg/s at \(v_e\) = 3,000 m/s produces exactly 3,000 N of thrust.
physicsChecks: Doubling the \(v_e\) slider doubles the displayed thrust at fixed \(\dot{m}\) (linear relationship visible in the readout).
With the tank at zero, firing produces 0 N and zero velocity change regardless of any other setting — the misconception check.
Total momentum is conserved: spacecraft momentum gain equals the summed momentum of ejected dots (shown as a balance readout).
Higher \(v_e\) at the same tank size yields visibly higher final spacecraft speed.

scenes.part: The trap: why moving in space is expensive
scenes.title: Every maneuver has a fixed price in velocity change, set by orbital mechanics
scenes.teaches: Orbits are velocity states, so each maneuver costs a fixed ΔV independent of engine choice — and a spacecraft's total ΔV budget is its lifetime.
scenes.caption: Changing orbit means changing velocity, and physics fixes the price no matter which engine pays it: ~100 m/s for a collision dodge, ~5,000 m/s for the full GEO run. Our spacecraft's ΔV budget is loaded at launch — when the meter empties, the vehicle is dead weight.
scenes.visualMetaphor: Left: Earth with two orbits drawn to scale — low Earth orbit at 400 km and geostationary orbit at 35,786 km — plus our 500 kg spacecraft in LEO. Right: a menu of maneuvers with ΔV price tags (collision dodge: ~100 m/s; modest repositioning: ~100 m/s; GEO run: ~5,000 m/s) and a vertical 'ΔV budget' fuel gauge. Clicking a maneuver animates the transfer arc and drains the gauge by exactly its price. Predict-before-reveal: 'How many 100 m/s dodges fit in the budget it takes to do one GEO run?' Answer: 50.

animatedVariable.name: selected maneuver
animatedVariable.range: collision dodge (100 m/s) | repositioning (100 m/s) | GEO run (5,000 m/s)
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: The chosen transfer arc animates on the orbit diagram and the ΔV budget gauge drops by that maneuver's fixed price.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: LEO-to-GEO transfer: ~5,000 m/s. Collision dodge: ~100 m/s. One GEO run = fifty dodges.
physicsChecks: The GEO run drains exactly 50× more of the gauge than one dodge.
Prices are identical regardless of any engine setting — ΔV cost is a property of the orbits, not the propulsion.
Repeating dodges eventually empties the gauge, after which maneuver buttons visibly fail (dead spacecraft).
GEO is drawn ~90× farther from Earth's surface than the LEO altitude (35,786 km vs 400 km).

scenes.part: The trap: why moving in space is expensive
scenes.title: Propellant needs grow exponentially with ΔV, so low exhaust velocity hits a hard ceiling
scenes.teaches: \(ΔV = v_e·\ln(m_{wet}/m_{dry})\) means the propellant fraction grows exponentially with target ΔV, capping a chemical 500 kg spacecraft near 700 m/s.
scenes.caption: The logarithm in \(ΔV = v_e·\ln(m_{wet}/m_{dry})\) is a tax: added propellant must itself be accelerated, so requirements grow exponentially. At \(v_e\) = 2,900 m/s the GEO run demands an 82%-propellant vehicle — and realistic storable-propellant tanks cap our spacecraft near 700 m/s total.
scenes.visualMetaphor: A slider sets target ΔV (0 – 6,000 m/s). The spacecraft is drawn as a bar split into dry mass (fixed 500 kg) and propellant, which grows as the slider moves; a curve plots propellant fraction vs ΔV, visibly exponential. A dashed line marks the realistic tank limit for a small spacecraft; where the curve crosses it (~700 m/s for storable chemical, \(v_e\) ≈ 2,200 m/s) the bar turns red: 'cannot be built.' Predict-before-reveal: 'To double ΔV from 700 to 1,400 m/s, does propellant double?' Reveal: the mass ratio squares — far more than double.

animatedVariable.name: target ΔV
animatedVariable.range: 0 – 6,000 m/s (toggle between \(v_e\) = 2,200 m/s storable and 2,900 m/s high-performance chemical)
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: The propellant segment of the mass bar grows exponentially, the mass-ratio readout \(m_{wet}/m_{dry} = e^{ΔV/v_e}\) climbs, and the bar turns red past the buildable tank limit.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: At \(v_e\) = 2,900 m/s, ΔV = 5,000 m/s requires \(m_{wet}/m_{dry} = e^{5000/2900} ≈ 5.6\) — an 82% propellant vehicle. Storable chemical caps a 500 kg spacecraft at ~700 m/s.
physicsChecks: Doubling the ΔV slider squares the displayed mass ratio (exponential, not linear, growth is visible in the curve's shape).
At ΔV = 0 the propellant fraction is exactly 0% (boundary behavior).
Switching to the higher-\(v_e\) toggle shifts the entire curve right: the same tank buys proportionally more ΔV.
The storable-chemical configuration crosses the buildable limit near 700 m/s, matching the paper's ceiling.

scenes.part: The trap: why moving in space is expensive
scenes.title: For 50 years the choice was high thrust or high efficiency — never both
scenes.teaches: On a thrust-vs-specific-impulse map, chemical and electric engines occupy opposite corners, and the corner our GEO run needs — high thrust AND high \(I_{sp}\) — is empty.
scenes.caption: Engineers quote efficiency as specific impulse, \(I_{sp} = v_e/g_0\), in seconds: 300 s means exhaust at ~2,940 m/s. Chemical engines (180–330 s, hundreds of newtons) die at 700 m/s. Hall thrusters (1,500 s) survive the GEO run, but at millinewtons — a million times less force — it takes 527 days.
scenes.visualMetaphor: A scatter plot: x-axis \(I_{sp}\) (0–2,000 s), y-axis thrust on a log scale (1 mN – 1 kN). Two labeled dots: 'chemical' (300 s, ~400 N) and 'Hall thruster / electric' (1,500 s, ~0.08 mN–mN class). The upper-right quadrant glows and is labeled 'empty for 50 years.' Predict-before-reveal: 'Pick an engine for the GEO run.' Clicking chemical stamps OUT OF PROPELLANT AT ~700 m/s across the orbit diagram; clicking electric stamps ARRIVES IN 527 DAYS.

animatedVariable.name: engine choice for the GEO run
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: The selected dot pulses and the GEO run verdict appears: chemical fails on ΔV budget (~700 m/s ceiling); electric succeeds on ΔV but the mission clock reads 527 days.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: Chemical: 180–330 s \(I_{sp}\), hundreds of N. Hall thruster: ~1,500 s \(I_{sp}\), millinewtons — roughly a million times less thrust.
physicsChecks: The thrust axis is log-scaled and the chemical–electric gap spans ~6 orders of magnitude.
Neither existing engine dot sits in the highlighted upper-right region — the gap is visibly empty.
Chemical's failure mode is ΔV (budget bar empties at ~700 m/s), electric's is time (clock, not budget) — two distinct visible causes.
\(I_{sp}\) = 300 s converts to \(v_e\) ≈ 2,940 m/s in the on-screen conversion readout.

scenes.part: The new engine: sunlight as the heat source
scenes.title: Exhaust velocity scales as √(T/M) — combustion locks both knobs, external heat frees them
scenes.teaches: \(v_e ∝ \sqrt{T/M}\): a nozzle converts thermal motion into directed flow, so hotter gas and lighter molecules both raise exhaust velocity — and only an external heat source lets you set T and M independently.
scenes.caption: Kinetic theory sets molecular speed at \(\sqrt{kT/m}\): hotter and lighter both mean faster, so \(v_e ∝ \sqrt{T/M}\). Combustion welds the two knobs together — burning fixes the temperature and produces heavy exhaust molecules. Heat the propellant externally instead, and you can pick pure hydrogen (M = 2) at 3,000 K for ~800 s \(I_{sp}\).
scenes.visualMetaphor: A chamber of jiggling molecules feeding a nozzle; molecule speed and exit-stream speed animate with the controls. Two sliders: temperature T (300–3,500 K) and molar mass M (2–44 g/mol, with labeled stops: H₂ = 2, NH₃ = 17, H₂O = 18, CO₂ = 44). An \(I_{sp}\) gauge responds as \(\sqrt{T/M}\). A 'combustion mode' toggle chains the sliders: turning it on snaps to T ≈ 3,300 K with heavy product molecules (M ≈ 18+), gauge stuck at chemical values. Predict-before-reveal: 'Combustion is off. Set M to hydrogen and T to 3,000 K — where does the gauge land?' Reveal: ~800 s, well past any chemical engine.

animatedVariable.name: chamber temperature T and propellant molar mass M
animatedVariable.range: T: 300–3,500 K; M: 2–44 g/mol; plus a combustion-mode toggle that locks them together
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: Molecules jiggle and exit faster, and the \(I_{sp}\) gauge moves as \(\sqrt{T/M}\); combustion mode visibly freezes both sliders at chemical values.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: Hydrogen (M = 2) at ~3,000 K: ~800 s \(I_{sp}\). Ammonia (M = 17): 350–450 s. Both beat chemical's 180–330 s without combustion.
physicsChecks: Quadrupling T doubles the \(I_{sp}\) gauge at fixed M (square-root scaling readable from two screenshots).
At fixed T, moving M from 18 to 2 raises \(I_{sp}\) by a factor of 3 (\(\sqrt{9}\)).
With combustion mode on, the gauge cannot exceed the chemical range (180–330 s) no matter what the reader tries — the lock is the visible cause.
Molecule animation speed rises with T and falls with M, matching the gauge direction.

scenes.part: The new engine: sunlight as the heat source
scenes.title: Mirrors focus sunlight onto a heat exchanger — the sun replaces the combustion chamber
scenes.teaches: Solar-thermal propulsion concentrates sunlight onto a 3D-printed heat exchanger that heats ammonia to ~3,000 K, producing 100–200 N of thrust at 350–450 s \(I_{sp}\) — chemical-class force at above-chemical efficiency.
scenes.caption: Follow the energy: deployable mirrors concentrate sunlight onto a monolithic heat exchanger. Ammonia flashes to ~3,000 K, and the nozzle turns that heat into 100–200 N of thrust at 350–450 s \(I_{sp}\). This is not solar panels driving an ion thruster — the sunlight becomes heat directly, which is why the thrust is a million times higher.
scenes.visualMetaphor: A labeled cutaway of our spacecraft, now fitted with the solar-thermal engine (Portal Space Systems' Supernova architecture): membrane mirrors → focused beam → 3D-printed refractory heat exchanger (internal channels drawn) → ammonia tank feeding through → nozzle. A step-through control lights up each stage in sequence with its number (concentration ratio, 3,000 K, 100–200 N). A comparison toggle swaps in the solar-ELECTRIC path (panels → electricity → ion thruster) with its thrust readout collapsing to millinewtons, making the distinction visible rather than asserted.

animatedVariable.name: energy-path walkthrough
animatedVariable.range: step 1 (mirrors) → step 2 (heat exchanger, 3,000 K) → step 3 (nozzle, 100–200 N); toggle: solar-thermal vs solar-electric path
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: Each step highlights one component with its operating numbers; the toggle reroutes the energy path through panels and drops the thrust readout by a factor of ~1,000,000.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: 100–200 N of thrust at 350–450 s \(I_{sp}\) with ammonia heated to ~3,000 K; hydrogen variant reaches ~800 s.
physicsChecks: The energy path in solar-thermal mode never passes through electricity — sunlight → heat → directed flow, visible in the highlighted route.
Toggling to solar-electric drops the thrust readout from ~150 N to millinewtons while \(I_{sp}\) rises to ~1,500 s — both change, in opposite directions.
There is no oxidizer tank anywhere in the diagram (no combustion), and the caption's 3,000 K appears at the heat exchanger, nowhere else.
The engine's plotted point lands in the previously empty upper-right region of the scene-4 thrust-vs-\(I_{sp}\) map (shown as an inset).

scenes.part: Head to head on the GEO run
scenes.title: Thrust sets the clock: the GEO run takes 9 hours, not 527 days
scenes.teaches: Maneuver time scales inversely with thrust, so 100–200 N finishes the 5,000 m/s GEO run in ~9 hours while a millinewton Hall thruster spirals for 12,600+ hours.
scenes.caption: Maneuver time scales inversely with thrust: at 100 m/s, solar-thermal burns for ~10 minutes while electric spirals for ~223 hours. Slide to the full 5,000 m/s GEO run and the gap becomes 9 hours versus 12,600 hours — 527 days, during which the reason for moving has long expired.
scenes.visualMetaphor: Split view. Left: the LEO-to-GEO orbit diagram with two spacecraft racing — solar-thermal executes a few short bright burn arcs with coasting, electric traces a slow continuous spiral. Right: a chart of maneuver time (log axis, 1 minute to 2 years) vs ΔV (100–5,000 m/s) with three curves: chemical (fast but grayed out and truncated at its 700 m/s ΔV ceiling), solar-thermal, electric. A ΔV slider moves a cursor along all curves. Predict-before-reveal at the start: 'Electric takes 223 hours for 100 m/s. Guess the time for 5,000 m/s before sliding.' The reveal — 527 days — lands against most readers' linear guess.

animatedVariable.name: maneuver ΔV
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: The race animation re-runs at the chosen ΔV and both mission clocks update: solar-thermal stays in minutes-to-hours, electric climbs into weeks-to-months, chemical grays out past 700 m/s.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: 5,000 m/s GEO run: ~9 hours solar-thermal vs >12,600 hours (527 days) electric. 100 m/s: ~10 minutes vs ~223 hours.
physicsChecks: At every slider position the solar-thermal curve sits below electric by roughly three orders of magnitude on the log time axis.
The chemical curve terminates at 700 m/s with a visible 'ΔV budget exhausted' marker — its failure mode stays distinct from electric's.
Both time readouts increase monotonically with ΔV; neither ever decreases.
The electric spiral animation visibly completes many orbits while solar-thermal's burn arcs occupy a small fraction of one transfer.

scenes.part: Head to head on the GEO run
scenes.title: Cheap propellant, reusable hardware, free energy: solar-thermal wins on cost at every ΔV
scenes.teaches: Maneuver cost decomposes into propellant + hardware + energy, and solar-thermal wins all three — $222k vs $1.46M (chemical) at 100 m/s, $9.1M vs $10.9M (electric) at 5,000 m/s.
scenes.caption: Speed usually costs money; here it doesn't. Ammonia runs $1/kg versus xenon at $2,500/kg, the hardware amortizes over 50+ restarts, and sunlight is free. The result: solar-thermal is cheapest at every ΔV — $222k vs $1.46M against chemical at 100 m/s, $9.1M vs $10.9M against electric on the full GEO run.
scenes.visualMetaphor: Three stacked bars (chemical, solar-thermal, electric), each split into propellant / hardware amortization / energy layers, above a ΔV slider. As ΔV grows, each layer grows at its own rate: electric's propellant layer (xenon, $2,500/kg) balloons despite high \(I_{sp}\); chemical's hardware layer dominates because each stage amortizes over few burns; solar-thermal's propellant layer stays nearly flat ($1/kg ammonia) and its energy layer reads $0 throughout. Predict-before-reveal: 'Electric is 5× more fuel-efficient — at 5,000 m/s, is it cheaper than solar-thermal?' Slide to reveal: no, $10.9M vs $9.1M.

animatedVariable.name: maneuver ΔV
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: The three stacked bars re-scale, each cost layer growing at its own rate; the total readouts hit $222k / $1.46M at 100 m/s and $9.1M / $10.9M at 5,000 m/s.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: At 100 m/s: solar-thermal ~$222k vs chemical $1.46M. At 5,000 m/s: solar-thermal ~$9.1M vs electric $10.9M. Ammonia: $1/kg; xenon: $2,500/kg.
physicsChecks: Solar-thermal's total bar is the shortest at both slider extremes (default 100 m/s and max 5,000 m/s).
Solar-thermal's energy layer reads $0 at every ΔV; both competitors' energy/consumable layers are nonzero.
Electric's propellant layer grows visibly faster than solar-thermal's as ΔV increases (the $2,500/kg vs $1/kg cause is labeled on the layers).
All cost totals increase monotonically with ΔV — no curve ever bends downward.

scenes.part: Head to head on the GEO run
scenes.title: Responsiveness requires sitting fueled for months and firing in minutes
scenes.teaches: Cryogenic propellants boil off in orbit while ammonia stores indefinitely, so solar-thermal is the only class that is simultaneously storable, high-thrust, and high-ΔV.
scenes.caption: A high-performance cryogenic stage can't wait — methalox boils off, so the response starts with a launch-and-fuel delay of 24 hours minimum. Ammonia sits as a liquid at modest pressure for months. When the alarm sounds at t = 0, solar-thermal fires within minutes and completes a 100 m/s response in ~10 minutes of burn.
scenes.visualMetaphor: A response-timeline race. At t = 0 an alert flag appears ('conjunction warning: 100 m/s response needed'). Four horizontal lanes advance with a play control: cryogenic chemical (methalox) shows a 24-hour 'launch + fuel' block before its burn can even start; storable chemical fires in minutes but its ΔV budget bar shows the maneuver consuming ~1/7 of its 700 m/s lifetime; electric fires immediately but its burn bar stretches ~223 hours; solar-thermal fires in minutes and finishes in ~10 minutes. A months-long pre-timeline strip shows each vehicle waiting: methalox's tank visibly drains (boil-off) while ammonia's stays full.

animatedVariable.name: mission clock
animatedVariable.range: t = −3 months (waiting on station) to t = +24 hours after the alert
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: During the waiting phase the cryogenic tank level falls while ammonia holds steady; after t = 0 each lane's burn progresses at its own rate, finish flags appearing in order: solar-thermal, storable chemical, then electric and methalox far off-screen.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: Methalox response: ≥24 hours before the burn begins. Solar-thermal: fueled for months, fires in minutes, 100 m/s complete in ~10 minutes.
physicsChecks: Only the ammonia tank level is unchanged between the t = −3 months and t = 0 screenshots; methalox visibly loses propellant to boil-off.
Solar-thermal's finish flag appears before any other lane's.
Storable chemical's ΔV budget bar drops by 100/700 ≈ 14% for this single maneuver, while solar-thermal's 5–6 km/s budget barely moves.
Electric's lane shows no pre-burn delay but the longest burn duration — delay and duration are separately visible causes.

scenes.title: The physics passed in 1979; the manufacturing, materials, and mirrors passed in the 2020s
scenes.teaches: Three independently matured technologies — monolithic 3D printing, 3,000 K refractory alloys, and >1,000 W/kg membrane mirrors — turned a validated concept into a buildable engine.
scenes.caption: Rockwell validated the physics in 1979 — ground tests even demonstrated >800 s \(I_{sp}\) with hydrogen — but the hardware was unbuildable. Flip each subsystem from 1980 to 2026. The temperature ceiling alone rises from 1,800 K to 3,000 K; since \(v_e ∝ \sqrt{T}\), that is a 29% \(I_{sp}\) gain before the other two fixes even land.
scenes.visualMetaphor: Three side-by-side subsystem cards, each with a 1980/2026 toggle. Heat exchanger: hundreds of brazed machined parts (leak points highlighted) vs a single monolithic 3D-printed refractory component with computationally optimized channels. Materials: a thermometer capped at 1,800 K ('structural failure') vs 3,000 K, wired to a live \(I_{sp}\) gauge that moves as \(\sqrt{T}\) (×\(\sqrt{3000/1800}\) ≈ ×1.29). Mirrors: a heavy rigid dish vs a membrane reflector with a W/kg readout jumping past 1,000 W/kg (10× conventional solar arrays), feeding a rocket-equation mass-budget bar that flips from 'does not close' to 'closes.' Predict-before-reveal: 'Which of the three was the physics problem?' Reveal: none — all three are engineering.

animatedVariable.name: technology era per subsystem
animatedVariable.range: 1980 ↔ 2026, independently for each of the three cards
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: Each flipped card swaps its hardware drawing and numbers; only with all three at 2026 does the overall verdict banner switch from 'concept shelved' to 'flight vehicle buildable.'

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: Temperature ceiling 1,800 K → ~3,000 K gives \(\sqrt{3000/1800}\) ≈ 1.29× exhaust velocity; membrane mirrors exceed 1,000 W/kg, 10× conventional solar arrays.
physicsChecks: The \(I_{sp}\) gauge rises by ~29% when only the materials card flips (square-root scaling, verifiable from the two screenshots).
The 'buildable' banner requires all three toggles at 2026 — any single 1980 setting keeps it red.
The 1979 Rockwell validation and >800 s hydrogen ground-test note are displayed in the 1980 state, making clear the physics was never the barrier.
The mirror card's mass-budget bar only closes above the 1,000 W/kg threshold.

scenes.title: 1.2 million filed satellites turn maneuvering from an edge case into infrastructure
scenes.teaches: Filings now exceed 1.2 million satellites against ~15,000 active today, making collision avoidance, repositioning, and de-orbit continuous operations rather than rare events.
scenes.caption: Responsive ΔV was never built because it was never needed: ~15,000 active satellites mostly parked in fixed slots for 15 years. Filings in the last 18 months exceed 1.2 million — over 95,000 broadband satellites, SpaceX's 1,000,000-satellite compute filing, Blue Origin's 51,600. At that density, every spacecraft becomes our scene-2 spacecraft, dodging and repositioning continuously.
scenes.visualMetaphor: A log-scale population chart with stacked contributions: today's ~15,000 active satellites as a thin baseline sliver, then buttons that add each filed layer — mega-constellation broadband (>95,000), SpaceX orbital compute (1,000,000, filed January 2026), Project Sunrise (51,600), other filings — climbing past 1.2 million. Beside it, an orbital-shell visualization densifies with each addition, and a counter converts density into operations: expected conjunction warnings per satellite per year rising from 'rare' to 'routine.' Predict-before-reveal: 'Today is 15,000. Guess the filed total before pressing the buttons.'

animatedVariable.name: satellite population layers
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: Each button stacks a labeled layer on the log-scale bar and visibly densifies the orbital shell; the maneuvers-per-year counter climbs with density.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: ~15,000 active satellites today; >1.2 million filed in 18 months, including SpaceX's 1,000,000-satellite compute filing (January 2026) and Project Sunrise's 51,600.
physicsChecks: The filed total exceeds the active population by roughly 80× (two orders of magnitude, readable on the log axis).
The compute-satellite layer alone dwarfs all other layers combined (1,000,000 of the 1.2M+).
The conjunction/maneuver counter increases monotonically with each added layer — density is the visible cause of demand.
The baseline sliver remains visible at all zoom states so the before/after contrast survives both screenshots.

scenes.title: Nuclear-thermal is the same engine with a denser heat source
scenes.teaches: Downstream of the heat source, solar-thermal and nuclear-thermal are identical — the same \(v_e ∝ \sqrt{T/M}\), the same heat exchanger — so today's hardware is ~90% of nuclear performance at ~10% of the cost, and carries over when regulation permits reactors.
scenes.caption: Flip the heat source and nothing downstream changes: mirrors out, reactor in, and the same 3D-printed heat exchanger and \(v_e ∝ \sqrt{T/M}\) physics keep working — now free of the sunlight-availability constraint. Solar-thermal today delivers ~90% of nuclear-thermal performance at ~10% of the cost, while building exactly the flight heritage a 2030s nuclear engine will need.
scenes.visualMetaphor: The scene-6 cutaway returns with a single toggle on the heat source. Solar mode: mirrors focus sunlight on the heat exchanger; a small constraint badge reads 'needs sunlight.' Nuclear mode: a reactor block replaces the mirrors; the badge clears; the propellant path, heat exchanger, and nozzle are drawn identically and highlighted 'unchanged.' Side readouts compare performance (~90% in solar mode relative to nuclear) and cost (~10%). Predict-before-reveal: 'How many downstream components change when you flip the source?' Reveal: zero — every unchanged part flashes green.

animatedVariable.name: heat source
animatedVariable.whatChangesOnScreen: Only the heat-source block swaps; the heat exchanger, propellant feed, and nozzle flash green as 'unchanged,' and the performance/cost readouts shift to ~100% performance / ~10× cost in nuclear mode.

scenes.quantitativeAnchor: Solar-thermal achieves ~90% of nuclear-thermal performance at ~10% of the cost; nuclear flight approval is expected in the 2030s.
physicsChecks: Between the two toggle states, the only differing pixels in the engine diagram are the heat-source block and the sunlight-availability badge.
The governing equation \(v_e ∝ \sqrt{T/M}\) is displayed identically in both modes — same physics regime, not a new one.
Performance and cost readouts move in opposite directions across the toggle (~90%→100% performance, ~10%→100% cost), matching the paper's ratios.
The heat exchanger carries a 'carries over intact' label in both states, tying back to the scene-10 manufacturing story.
